questions.question: What happens when you modify a quick filter in a shared database view but do not click “Save for everyone”?

options.text: The changes are applied to everyone immediately.
options.explanation: Changes are not shared until you explicitly save for everyone.

options.text: Only you see the filter changes; other team members keep the previous view.
options.explanation: Quick filter changes are private until you click “Save for everyone,” so only you see them.

options.text: The view reverts to the default filter settings for everyone.
options.explanation: The view does not revert; it simply remains unchanged for others.

options.text: Notion shows an error and discards the changes.
options.explanation: No error occurs; the changes are just unsaved for the team.

questions.question: When creating a new database or view, what new option can you select?

options.text: A pre‑made template from the template gallery.
options.explanation: Templates are still available, but the new option is about data sources.

options.text: An existing data source from your workspace.
options.explanation: The article explains that you can now select an existing data source to sync content.

options.text: A custom color scheme for the database.
options.explanation: Color schemes are unrelated to the new feature.

options.text: A permission level for who can edit the database.
options.explanation: Permissions are managed elsewhere; they are not the new selection option.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the purpose of the unified “View options” menu?

options.text: To combine all database customization tools—layout, properties, filter, sort—into one place.
options.explanation: The unified menu consolidates these options for easier access.

options.text: To automatically generate a new view for every team member.
options.explanation: The menu does not create views automatically.

options.text: To replace the ability to use tabs in a database.
options.explanation: Tabs remain a separate feature; the menu does not replace them.

options.text: To sync databases across different Notion workspaces.
options.explanation: Cross‑workspace syncing is not mentioned as part of this menu.

questions.question: Why might a team choose to use an existing data source instead of creating a new database for meeting notes?

options.text: It reduces redundancy and makes it easier to maintain a single source of truth.
options.explanation: Using one source and creating views avoids duplicate databases and improves clarity.

options.text: It allows each team member to have a personalized version of the notes.
options.explanation: Personalized versions would increase, not reduce, duplication.

options.text: It automatically archives old meeting notes after 30 days.
options.explanation: Archiving is not a function of using an existing data source.

options.text: It disables the ability to filter notes by participant.
options.explanation: Filtering remains available; using a single source does not remove this capability.

questions.question: What advantage do tabs provide when displaying multiple views inside a single block?

options.text: Tabs automatically merge data from different sources into one row.
options.explanation: Tabs simply switch between views; they do not merge rows.

options.text: Tabs let users quickly switch between views without navigating away from the page.
options.explanation: The article highlights that tabs make it easier to find and switch between important views.

options.text: Tabs hide all but the first view to improve page load speed.
options.explanation: Tabs do not hide content for performance; they organize visibility.

options.text: Tabs enforce read‑only access to the underlying data.
options.explanation: Permissions are independent of the tab UI.

questions.question: Which statement accurately reflects the new behavior of filter groups in Notion databases?

options.text: Filter groups are now the default filter type for all new views.
options.explanation: The article says filter groups are no longer the default.

options.text: Filter groups are hidden under the “advanced” filter option but still available when needed.
options.explanation: They remain accessible via the advanced filter menu.

options.text: Filter groups have been removed entirely from Notion.
options.explanation: They still exist; they are just not shown by default.

options.text: Filter groups now apply globally to all databases in a workspace.
options.explanation: Filter groups are still view‑specific, not global.

questions.question: When would it be most appropriate to sync an existing data source rather than create a new database?

options.text: When you need a completely isolated set of data that no one else should see.
options.explanation: Isolation suggests creating a separate database, not syncing.

options.text: When you want to consolidate related information and reuse the same properties across teams.
options.explanation: Consolidation and shared properties are the primary reasons given.

options.text: When you plan to delete the original database after a month.
options.explanation: Syncing does not address deletion; it would just create a dependent view.

options.text: When you need to export the data to CSV daily.
options.explanation: Exporting is unrelated to the decision to sync a data source.

questions.question: How do quick filters improve collaboration compared to the previous filter system?

options.text: They allow each user to make temporary filter changes that only affect them until saved for everyone.
options.explanation: Quick filters are private until the user clicks “Save for everyone,” supporting collaborative control.

options.text: They automatically synchronize filter changes across all team members in real time.
options.explanation: Changes are not shared automatically; they require explicit saving.

options.text: They replace the need for any sort or grouping options.
options.explanation: Sorting and grouping remain available; quick filters are an addition.

options.text: They lock the view for other users while one person is editing filters.
options.explanation: Views remain editable by others; quick filters do not lock the view.

questions.question: What is a key benefit of being able to copy a view from the original data source when creating a new linked instance?

options.text: It eliminates the need to recreate filters, sorts, and other customizations manually.
options.explanation: The article emphasizes that view options are automatically applied, saving effort.

options.text: It merges the original view’s data with the new view’s data into a single table.
options.explanation: Copying a view does not merge data; it replicates the view settings.

options.text: It grants edit permissions to all users of the new linked instance.
options.explanation: Permissions are not altered by copying a view.

options.text: It automatically updates the original view whenever the new view is edited.
options.explanation: Changes to the new view do not affect the original view; only the view settings are copied initially.

questions.question: Which layout options are now accessible through the unified “View options” menu?

options.text: Table, Calendar, Timeline, List, Board, Gallery.
options.explanation: These are the layout types listed in the article under the View options menu.

options.text: Table, Spreadsheet, Kanban, Mind Map.
options.explanation: Spreadsheet and Mind Map are not mentioned as layout options.

options.text: List, Board, Gantt, Diagram.
options.explanation: Gantt and Diagram are not part of the listed layouts.

options.text: Calendar, Timeline, Flowchart, Dashboard.
options.explanation: Flowchart and Dashboard are not provided as layout choices.
